title: Understanding Elastic Collisions
text: In an elastic collision, both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved. This means that the total kinetic energy before the collision is equal to the total kinetic energy after the collision.

title: Using Conservation of Momentum
text: The conservation of momentum can be expressed as: $$m1 v1_{initial}+m2 v2_{initial}=m1 v1_{final}+m2 v2_{final}$$. Here, m1 and m2 are the masses of the alpha particle and uranium nucleus, respectively, and v1 and v2 are their velocities.

title: Using Conservation of Kinetic Energy
text: The conservation of kinetic energy can be expressed as: $$0.5m1 {v1_{initial}}^2+0.5m2 {v2_{initial}}^2=0.5m1 {v1_{final}}^2+0.5m2 {v2_{final}}^2$$. Since the uranium nucleus is initially stationary, $$v2_{initial}=0$$.

title: Calculating the Final Velocities
text: Using the conservation equations, solve for the final velocities of the alpha particle and the uranium nucleus.

title: Finding the Kinetic Energy Transferred
text: Calculate the kinetic energy transferred to the uranium nucleus using the formula: $$KE_{transferred}=0.5m2 {v2_{final}}^2$$.

title: Calculating the Percent of Kinetic Energy Transferred
text: The percent of kinetic energy transferred is given by: $$100\frac{KE_{transferred}}{KE_{initial}}$$, where $$KE_{initial}$$ is the initial kinetic energy of the alpha particle.
